scores.materiality_reasoning: Protection from conviction directly preserves bodily freedom and prevents material penalties like fines or execution.
scores.generality_reasoning: Applies to all criminal and civil jury verdicts, a broad legal category.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Specifically concerns the outcome of a single trial.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Being a defendant is an episodic role tied to a specific legal proceeding.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Aristotle describes the functioning machinery of the Athenian courts in his own time.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Presented as a standard procedural rule of the Athenian constitution.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being a defendant does not inherently imply winning in a tie; this is a specific legal choice.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Direct payment in cash (obols) is a tangible material resource.
scores.generality_reasoning: Specific to the institution of the jury system.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Payment is received per session/service day.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Juror is an episodic role assigned by lot.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Describes the current Athenian administrative practice.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: A purely descriptive account of how jurors are compensated.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Serving as a juror does not conceptually guarantee being paid; it is a policy.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Eligibility for high office grants access to political power and economic perks.
scores.generality_reasoning: The Archonship was the foundational executive office of Athens.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Eligibility is a long-term status within a career path, though the office term is finite.
scores.immutability_reasoning: The Eupatridae are a hereditary noble lineage.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: The text refers to the transition in 682 B.C., centuries before Aristotle.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Reported as a historical/constitutional fact.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Nobility is defined by birth, not necessarily by holding the office of Archon.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Exclusion from high office limits material influence and career advancement.
scores.generality_reasoning: Covers high magistracies, a major societal axis.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Exclusion persists as long as one remains in the lowest wealth bracket.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Wealth brackets (Thetes) can change, though for most it was a lifelong status.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Referencing the era of Solonian or post-Solonian classes and Anthemion's lineage.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Uses archaeological/inscriptional evidence to report historical legal restrictions.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being poor (Thete) does not definitionally include exclusion from office; it's a legal rule.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Gaining eligibility for the archonship provides access to state power.
scores.generality_reasoning: The archonship was a central broad-category right.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Constitutes a permanent expansion of political rights for the group.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Zeugites were defined by a wealth census, which was relatively stable but changeable.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Refers specifically to the year 457 B.C., over a century before Aristotle.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Factual reporting of a dated legal reform.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Wealth level does not inherently equate to holding the office of Archon.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Daily wages (obols) are a direct material payment.
scores.generality_reasoning: Covers attendance at the Assembly, the primary political body.
scores.persistence_reasoning: A recurring right throughout one's adult life as a citizen.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Citizenship was a hard-to-change legal birth-status.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Refers to 392 B.C., several decades before Aristotle's writing.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Analysis of historically verifiable changes in the rate of pay.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Citizenship is not defined by receiving assembly pay.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Exercise of citizenship grants legal standing and voting rights.
scores.generality_reasoning: Citizenship is the foundational axis of Athenian legal/social life.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Status is held for the duration of adult life.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Group is defined by lineage/birth parents.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: This was the active, standard law in Aristotle's Athens.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Stated as a simple, objective legal criterion.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being a citizen is here defined by having citizen parents; the rule restates membership criteria.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Concerns the ownership and transmission of landed estates (property).
scores.generality_reasoning: Inheritance is a foundational structural feature of society.
scores.persistence_reasoning: The status of an epikleros and its effect on property is a lifelong structural arrangement.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Gender and familial position (lack of brothers) are immutable.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Describes current Athenian family law.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Factual description of legal institutions.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being a woman or daughter does not naturally entail being unable to inherit property.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Direct financial support/provision of capital for marriage.
scores.generality_reasoning: Specific to the scenario of fatherless daughters in poor families.
scores.persistence_reasoning: A dowry is a lifecycle-defining material asset for a woman.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Defined by gender and lineage position.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Refers to active protections overseen by the Archon in Aristotle's time.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Objectively reports the legal obligations of kin and state.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Kinship does not conceptually require providing a dowry; it is a legal rule.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Mandatory private funding of naval/cultural works is a heavy material cost.
scores.generality_reasoning: Liturgies were a major broad category of civic tax/duty.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Burdens were assigned periodically/annually.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Based on wealth brackets, which are durable but changeable.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Refers to the active tax system in Athens.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Describes established public law.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being rich does not inherently mean paying for a ship by law.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Right to prosecute officials can lead to material fines or recovery of stolen state assets.
scores.generality_reasoning: Oversight of all public officials is a broad political right.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Right held throughout one's adult citizenship.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Citizenship is a hard-to-change legal status.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Standard part of the contemporary Athenian 'euthunai' process.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Direct description of constitutional procedure.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Citizenship does not conceptually include the right to impeach every officer.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Specific legal guardianship ensuring maintenance of property and welfare.
scores.generality_reasoning: Specific to the recurring category of orphaned heiresses.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Protections last until male heirs reach legal maturity.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Based on biological sex and lineage position.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Describes active duties of the Athenian Archon.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Factual reporting of state care.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being an heiress does not inherently grant state protection.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Lack of legal independence forces reliance on others for property and defense.
scores.generality_reasoning: Fundamental restriction on legal personhood for all non-citizens.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Required throughout the duration of residency.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Resident alien (metic) status is durable but one can leave or be naturalized.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Active Athenian law for metics.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Stated as a mandatory legal requirement.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being an alien doesn't logically require a patron; it’s a specific Athenian law.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Exemption from military service but obligation to perform judicial labor.
scores.generality_reasoning: Tied to a specific recurring civic role (Arbitrator).
scores.persistence_reasoning: Limited strictly to one's 60th year (the 59th year cycle).
scores.immutability_reasoning: Age is immutable.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Describes current Athenian civic duties.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Standard descriptive report of administrative law.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Reaching age 59 does not definitionally include judicial duties.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Exchange of total property/assets is a drastic material transaction.
scores.generality_reasoning: Highly specific procedural technicality in litigation.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Concerns a single episode of liturgy assignment.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Wealth can fluctuate, making one eligible/ineligible over time.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: The law of antidosis was active in Aristotle's time.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Stated as a standard legal option.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being wealthy does not entail property exchange rules.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Labor obligation involves the physical body and lack of ownership of time.
scores.generality_reasoning: Slavery is a foundational axis of ancient society.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Regarded as a lifelong / 'natural' state.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Slavery is a hard-to-change legal and (here) supposedly natural status.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Aristotle is theorizing about the nature of the society he lives in.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Deeply theoretical/philosophical argument rather than a report of a statute.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Aristotle defines the slave group by the very fact that they are tools for labor.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Right to self-governance determines political and material independence.
scores.generality_reasoning: Broad framing of who is fit to rule or be ruled.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Presented as an inherent, lifelong quality of ethnicities.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Ethnicity/Barbarian status is birth-fixed.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Reflects contemporary Greek cultural and political ideology.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Purely rhetorical/normative claim based on poetic interpretation.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being a 'barbarian' (non-Greek) does not definitionally mean being a slave.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Lack of legal standing within the household limits control over movement and property.
scores.generality_reasoning: Patriarchal authority is a foundational structural feature of the home.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Lasts throughout minority or until the patriarch's death.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Based on age and lineage position.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Describes the traditional and current Greek domestic structure.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Blends observation of household norms with literary/Homeric justification.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Minors/sons are here defined by their subjection to the household head.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Total lack of self-ownership; defined as a material object.
scores.generality_reasoning: Fundamental status definition of a major social class.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Lifelong structural status.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Legal status of slavery is hard to change (requires manumission).
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Describes the contemporary economic system.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Philosophical definition used to argue for the nature of property.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Definitionally, a slave is a piece of property; the rule just restates the definition.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Complete loss of self-ownership and autonomy.
scores.generality_reasoning: Slavery is a foundational societal axis.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Lifelong/structural status.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Legal birth-status (or capture), hard to reverse.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Reflects the author's contemporary social reality.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Analytical definition for the purpose of philosophical classification.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Directly restates the definition of a slave as property belonging to another.
